questions.assertion: The function f: R → R defined by f(x) = 3x is one-one.
questions.reason: Since f(x1) = f(x2) implies 3x1 = 3x2, which further implies x1 = x2.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The function f is one-one as it satisfies the condition f(x1) = f(x2) implies x1 = x2

questions.assertion: The function f: R → R defined by f(x) = 3x is onto.
questions.reason: Since for every y ∈ R, there exists x = y/3 in R such that f(x) = f(y/3) = 3(y/3) = y.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The function f is onto as for every y ∈ R, there exists x = y/3 in R such that f(x) = y

questions.assertion: The function f: R → R defined by f(x) = x2 is one-one.
questions.reason: This statement is false because f(x) = f(-x) but x ≠ -x.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The function f is not one-one as f(x) = f(-x) but x ≠ -x

questions.assertion: The function f: R → R defined by f(x) = x2 is onto.
questions.reason: Since for every y ∈ R, there exists x = ±√y in R such that f(x) = f(±√y) = (√y)2 = y.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The function f is onto as for every y ∈ R, there exists x = ±√y in R such that f(x) = y

questions.assertion: If a function f: A → B is one-one, then it is onto.
questions.reason: This statement is false because a function can be one-one but not onto, e.g., f: R → R defined by f(x) = x2.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: This statement is false as a function can be one-one but not onto

questions.assertion: If a function f: A → B is onto, then it is one-one.
questions.reason: This statement is false because a function can be onto but not one-one, e.g., f: R → R defined by f(x) = x.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: This statement is false as a function can be onto but not one-one

questions.assertion: A relation R on A is symmetric if (a, b) ∈ R implies (b, a) ∈ R.
questions.reason: This statement is true because if (a, b) ∈ R, then by definition of symmetry, (b, a) ∈ R.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: This statement is true as it defines the property of a symmetric relation

questions.assertion: A relation R on A is transitive if (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R implies (a, c) ∈ R.
questions.reason: This statement is true because if (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R, then by definition of transitivity, (a, c) ∈ R.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: This statement is true as it defines the property of a transitive relation

questions.assertion: A relation R on A is reflexive if (a, a) ∈ R for all a ∈ A.
questions.reason: This statement is true because by definition of reflexivity, (a, a) ∈ R for all a ∈ A.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: This statement is true as it defines the property of a reflexive relation
